one_line_takeaway: Staged-Competence -- a curriculum-learning method combining a model-dependent 'preference alignment margin' difficulty score with staged reference-model updates and within-stage competence-based sampling -- cuts DPO safety alignment's OOD harmful-response rate by ~16% and jailbreak (Prefill/GCG) attack success rate by ~20% on average across LLaMA-3-8B, Qwen3-8B and Yi-1.5-9B, without degrading general capability or inducing over-refusal, and its advantage over standard DPO grows with model scale.
key_finding: Averaged across LLaMA-3-8B, Qwen3-8B, and Yi-1.5-9B, Staged-Competence reduces OOD harmful response rate by 16% and jailbreak attack success rate by 20% relative to Standard DPO, while matching or slightly improving in-distribution reward accuracy (91.3% / 89.6% / 88.2% for the three models vs. 89.8% / 86.7% / 85.5% for Standard DPO) and achieving roughly 3x greater reward-margin separation. Per-model OOD improvements (avg. harmful response rate, points below Standard DPO baseline) were 12 (LLaMA-3-8B), 29 (Qwen3-8B), and 7 (Yi-1.5-9B); per-attack improvements included Qwen3-8B Prefill -36pp and GCG -19pp, LLaMA-3-8B Prefill -22pp and GCG -15pp, Yi-1.5-9B Prefill -17pp and GCG -10pp. Staged-Competence beat the strongest curriculum baseline, Curri-DPO, by 9 points on OOD and 11 points on attacks on average. General capability (MMLU/HellaSwag) stayed within ~2-3 points of Standard DPO and XSTest over-refusal remained at or near zero (up to ~3.6% on Yi-1.5-9B). Token-level suppression analysis showed Staged-Competence's safety effect is not confined to the first few tokens (unlike the shallow-alignment phenomenon reported by Qi et al. 2025) but is roughly 3x stronger in total suppression and sustained across the full 128-token window. Scaling experiments on Qwen3 (1.7B/4B/8B) showed the safety advantage of Staged-Competence over Standard DPO widens with model size (OOD harmful-rate reduction of 1.5pp at 1.7B, 12.7pp at 4B, 28.9pp at 8B; attack-success reduction of 4.9pp at 1.7B, 26.0pp at 4B, 27.1pp at 8B), while Staged-Competence itself stays roughly flat (2-8% OOD, 8-12% attack ASR) as Standard DPO degrades with scale. Data-efficiency results: at 75% of training data, Staged-Competence matches or exceeds Standard DPO trained on 100% of the data on all three OOD benchmarks for both LLaMA-3-8B and Qwen3-8B; at 50% data the result is model-dependent (still beats Standard-DPO-100% on LLaMA-3-8B but underperforms it on Qwen3-8B).
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution is Staged-Competence, a curriculum-learning framework for DPO-based safety alignment that combines two mechanisms into one method: (1) a global, model-dependent difficulty score called the 'preference alignment margin' m_i = cos(e_yhat, e_y+) - cos(e_yhat, e_y-), computed by comparing the unaligned base model's zero-shot response to the provided safe/unsafe responses via all-MiniLM-L6-v2 sentence embeddings, used to sort the whole preference dataset easy-to-hard; and (2) a training algorithm (Algorithm 1) that partitions the sorted data into K=3 difficulty buckets/stages, updates the reference policy between stages (pi_ref^(k+1) = pi^(k), as in prior work Curri-DPO), and additionally applies competence-based sampling (a growing eligible pool per Platanios et al.'s square-root schedule) WITHIN each stage -- which the authors identify as missing from Curri-DPO, whose within-stage examples are merely randomly shuffled. The paper also constructs and releases a cleaned dataset, Cleaned-PKU-HH-SafeRLHF, by using a GPT-4o-mini judge to filter PKU-SafeRLHF and the single-turn subset of HH-RLHF down to pairs with a genuinely safe chosen response and unsafe rejected response, after finding that 82.2% of PKU-SafeRLHF 'chosen' responses were actually unsafe and 87.2% of HH-RLHF 'rejected' responses were actually safe.
threat_model: The paper evaluates robustness of DPO-safety-aligned LLMs against two jailbreak attack methods applied post-alignment: (1) the Prefill attack (Andriushchenko et al. 2025), which forces the model to begin its response with the first k=20 tokens of a known harmful completion (drawn from the DOOR dataset) before letting it generate freely, bypassing initial-token refusal; and (2) GCG (Zou et al. 2023), where a 20-token adversarial suffix is optimized via greedy token substitution (nanogcg, 500 steps, search width 512) against a non-abliterated vanilla base model and then transferred (applied without re-optimization) to the fine-tuned/aligned model variants, on 398 HarmBench behaviors spanning cyberattacks, bioweapons, and illegal activity. Out-of-distribution generalization of refusal behavior is also tested (not an adversarial attack per se) via AdvBench, SorryBench, and HEx-PHI, with harmful outputs judged by GPT-4o-mini. The threat model is therefore: an attacker with black-box query access who can prefix a model's generation or transfer a suffix optimized on a related unaligned model, attempting to elicit harmful content from a model that has undergone DPO-based safety training.
limitations: Explicitly noted by the authors: experiments are restricted to LoRA fine-tuning at the 8B parameter range (with a smaller-scale Qwen3 1.7B/4B/8B study); full fine-tuning and substantially larger models are left to future work. All safety judgments (dataset cleaning, harmful-response classification, attack-success classification) rely on a single GPT-4o-mini judge, which the paper does not independently validate against human annotation. The competence-based curriculum and staged reference updates add hyperparameters (K=3 stages, initial competence c0=0.01, epochs per stage) that were tuned per model (e.g., Yi-1.5-9B needed 4 rather than 5 epochs/stage to avoid preference over-optimization), suggesting some sensitivity to these choices. The 50%-data-efficiency result is inconsistent across models (helps LLaMA-3-8B, hurts Qwen3-8B), so data-efficiency gains are not guaranteed. The difficulty-scoring embedding model (all-MiniLM-L6-v2) truncates inputs beyond 256 tokens, which the authors acknowledge could affect ordering for longer responses, though they argue safety-relevant content is typically front-loaded. Curriculum learning here is applied only to safety alignment via DPO; the claim that it generalizes to other alignment objectives and non-safety domains is stated as future work, not demonstrated.

datasets_used: Anthropic HH-RLHF single-turn subset (49,388 raw pairs)
Cleaned-PKU-HH-SafeRLHF (paper's own cleaned/combined dataset: 10,931 pairs after GPT-4o-mini filtering, split 8,744 train / 2,187 test)
HarmBench (398 behaviors used for GCG attack generation)
DOOR dataset (used to build Prefill-attack prompts, 400 prompts evaluated)
quantitative_results: OOD harmful response rate improvement over Standard DPO (avg across 3 benchmarks): -12pp LLaMA-3-8B, -29pp Qwen3-8B, -7pp Yi-1.5-9B
Attack success rate improvement over Standard DPO: LLaMA-3-8B Prefill -22pp / GCG -15pp; Qwen3-8B Prefill -36pp / GCG -19pp; Yi-1.5-9B Prefill -17pp / GCG -10pp
In-distribution DPO reward accuracy: Staged-Competence 91.3% (LLaMA-3-8B), 89.6% (Qwen3-8B), 88.2% (Yi-1.5-9B) vs. Standard DPO 89.8%, 86.7%, 85.5%
Reward margin separation ~3x larger for Staged-Competence than Standard DPO across all three models
Total per-token suppression (sum of delta(t)) ~3x larger for Staged-Competence than Standard DPO

PKU-SafeRLHF: 82.2% of 'chosen' responses were actually unsafe; HH-RLHF: 87.2% of 'rejected' responses were actually safe; overall dataset retention after cleaning 11.8% (16.0% PKU-SafeRLHF, 8.0% HH-RLHF)

Data efficiency: Staged-Competence at 75% of training data matches or exceeds Standard DPO at 100% data on all three OOD benchmarks for both LLaMA-3-8B and Qwen3-8B (e.g., Table 6: LLaMA-3-8B Staged-Comp 75% Sorry/Adv/HEx = 16.4/4.4/10.0 vs Standard DPO 100% = 28.0/18.7/24.0)
Scaling with Qwen3 model size (1.7B/4B/8B): OOD harmful-rate reduction vs Standard DPO = 1.5pp / 12.7pp / 28.9pp; attack success rate reduction = 4.9pp / 26.0pp / 27.1pp
MMLU/HellaSwag quality preserved within ~2-3 points of Standard DPO; XSTest over-refusal at or near 0% on LLaMA-3-8B and Qwen3-8B, up to 3.6% on Yi-1.5-9B (Table 5)
baselines_compared: Standard DPO (single stage, random shuffle, fixed reference)
Sequential curriculum (single stage, fixed easy-to-hard order, fixed reference)
Sqrt-Competence (Platanios et al. 2019 competence-based sampling, single stage, fixed reference)
Curri-DPO (Pattnaik et al. 2024: K=3 stages with reference-model updates, random shuffle within stage) -- identified in the paper as the closest prior work, not the paper's own contribution
Unaligned base model (abliterated, no safety training)
